questions.q: Which desktop-environment pairing matches the guide?
options: GNOME is based on GTK+; KDE is based on Qt and suits users familiar with Windows
GNOME is based on Qt; KDE is based on GTK+
Both are based on GTK+ but KDE adds Qt on top
GNOME is a command-line shell; KDE is graphical
questions.why: GNOME 3 uses the GNOME Shell concept (not to be confused with the command-line shell).

questions.q: What is the relationship between free software and open-source software?
options: Free software is necessarily open-source, but the opposite is not true
Open-source software is necessarily free software
They are two names for exactly the same thing
Free software forbids selling; open source allows it - that is the only difference
questions.why: Open source = the source is available under certain conditions; that is distinct from the freedom offered by the GPL. And 'free' does not mean free of charge.

questions.q: Which of these is NOT one of the four freedoms the GPL implies?
options: The freedom to use the software without ever paying anyone
The freedom to run the program, for any purpose
The freedom to study how the program works and adapt it to your needs
The freedom to redistribute copies
questions.why: The fourth freedom is improving the program and publishing improvements. GPL products CAN have a cost - not for the product, but for the guarantee that developers keep evolving and supporting it.

questions.q: In the guide's 'Areas of use', which pairing is correct?
options: Security: gateway, firewall, router, proxy
Servers: banks, insurance, industry
Embedded systems: HTTP, email, groupware
Central computers: routers, internet boxes, SmartTVs
questions.why: Servers: HTTP/email/groupware/file sharing. Central computers: banks/insurance/industry. Embedded: routers/internet boxes/SmartTVs.

questions.q: The guide calls the shell a 'command interface'. What else does it stress the shell offers?
options: A real programming language - loops, alternatives, variables - for scripts that automate actions
A graphical settings panel for every service
Direct hardware access that bypasses the kernel
Automatic translation of Windows commands
questions.why: Scripts automate things like backups, creation of users, and system monitoring.

questions.q: Which shell descriptions match the guide's examples?
options: sh - the POSIX standard shell; csh - command-oriented shell in C; bash - Bourne-Again Shell, the Linux shell
sh - the Linux shell; csh - the POSIX standard; bash - a graphical shell
sh and bash are unrelated projects; csh is the POSIX standard
csh is the default Rocky Linux shell
questions.why: Several shells exist and are configurable per platform or user preference.

questions.q: After a successful login, per the 3.5.3 diagram, what does the shell display to say it is ready for a command?
options: A prompt - # or $
The word READY
A menu of available commands
The root password
questions.why: General message, local message, then the prompt: # (root) or $ (normal user).

questions.q: Besides pressing Ctrl+D, what command ends your shell session (per the 3.5.3 diagram)?
questions.why: The session loop runs prompt -> command -> result until CTRL+D or exit.
